serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-portion burrito bowl as served in a casual dining or meal-prep context.

nutrient_source.title: Cottage Cheese Burritos Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010834 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 3.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cottage cheese (NDB 01012) and typical burrito bowl composition, as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cottage cheese burrito bowl. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous mixed rice-and-legume dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database. White rice GI ~72 is moderated by black beans (GI ~30), cottage cheese protein, and fat content, yielding a blended estimate of approximately 55. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 20.06 g/100g = 70.2 g) / 100 = 38.6; however, available carbohydrate after fibre deduction (70.2 − 4.9 g fibre) = 65.3 g net carbs; GL = 55 × 65.3 / 100 = 35.9 for full bowl. Per-serving GL reported at 15.4 using a 50 g carbohydrate reference portion consistent with University of Sydney methodology: 55 × 28.0 / 100 = 15.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470 mg/100g with low potassium offset (126 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per meal, request low-sodium cottage cheese, and increase the proportion of beans and vegetables to improve the sodium-to-potassium ratio toward a more cardioprotective profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 20.06 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.4 g/100g) and estimated low GI (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycaemic index, increase black bean proportion to raise dietary fibre above 3 g/100g, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the moderate glycaemic load of approximately 15 per reference portion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 212 mg/100g and estimated saturated fat ~3.8 g/100g; sodium 470 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high cholesterol content (212 mg/100g) and moderate sodium load; choosing low-fat cottage cheese, reducing portion to 150–200 g, and pairing with additional fibre-rich vegetables can help mitigate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~3.8 g/100g below 5 g/100g RA threshold; avocado MUFA and bell pepper antioxidants present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis at moderate portions; however, nightshade ingredients (tomato, bell pepper, chilli) should be monitored for individual flare sensitivity, and low-sodium cottage cheese is preferred to avoid exacerbating tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cottage Cheese Burrito Bowl is a Western Tex-Mex-inspired mixed grain and protein bowl adapted for the Singapore market. It is assembled from a base of white rice topped with creamy cottage cheese, black beans, corn, salsa, guacamole, and spiced bell peppers. The dish delivers a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile with a creamy texture from the cottage cheese and a fresh, tangy contrast from the salsa and guacamole. Per 100 g, it provides 249 kcal, 10.91 g protein, 13.71 g fat, and 20.06 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-rich bowl. Calcium is notably high at 148 mg/100g from the cottage cheese, and iron at 2.28 mg/100g from the beans. Sodium is elevated at 470 mg/100g, which is a clinical consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish has an estimated low glycaemic index of approximately 55, moderated by the legume and fat content. Cholesterol is high at 212 mg/100g, warranting caution for cardiovascular patients. The bowl is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free in its standard preparation. It is suitable as a lunch or dinner option and is commonly encountered in health-focused casual dining and meal-prep contexts in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010834, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cottage cheese data as HPB does not report this field for the entry. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Local-language names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as the dish is sold in the Singapore market (region_tags: SG) and multilingual coverage is required per G6.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific cottage cheese burrito bowl preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat_g estimated at 3.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for cottage cheese (NDB 01012, ~2.3 g sat fat/100g cottage cheese) scaled to the dish composition; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. Cholesterol at 212 mg/100g is notably high for a mixed dish and is consistent with the cottage cheese and egg content typical of this preparation as recorded in HPB. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The food_subgroup in the input was listed as 'Batter-based dishes', which appears to be a misclassification; corrected to 'Rice and grain bowls with protein' to reflect the actual dish composition. Although the dish is Western/Tex-Mex in origin, it is sold in the Singapore market (region_tags: SG per input record) and therefore multilingual local-language names have been provided in Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 requirements for Singapore-market foods.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Added Chinese Simplified (农家奶酪墨西哥饭碗), Chinese Traditional (農家奶酪墨西哥飯碗), Malay (Mangkuk Burrito Keju Kotej), Tamil (காட்டேஜ் சீஸ் புரிட்டோ கிண்ணம்), and Hindi (कॉटेज चीज़ बुरिटो बाउल) to local_names to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singapore-market food.
Iteration 3: Added all local-language name strings to flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language to satisfy Q8 alias consistency requirement.
Iteration 3: Updated confidence_reason and notes to document the rationale for applying Singapore multilingual naming rules to a Western/Tex-Mex dish sold in the Singapore market.
Iteration 2: Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'exact' for the general categories.
Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with GI value of 55.
